name: Epoch-Based Reclamation

x.coordination: Threads enter a global epoch counter on each operation; retired nodes are tagged with the current epoch and freed only after all threads have advanced past it (no thread can still hold an old reference).
hazards: A stalled thread pins the epoch, blocking all reclamation (memory growth)
Choosing reclamation frequency vs memory use

def: A safe-memory-reclamation scheme that batches frees by global epochs: retired memory is reclaimed only once every thread has moved past the epoch in which it was retired.
problem: Lock-free structures need to free nodes safely without per-pointer bookkeeping; hazard pointers add per-access overhead.
mechanism: A global epoch advances periodically. Each thread announces the epoch it is operating in on entry. Retired nodes are queued by epoch and freed when all threads have observed a newer epoch, guaranteeing no live reference remains.
benefits: Very low per-operation overhead (vs hazard pointers)
limits: A stalled/blocked thread stalls reclamation → unbounded memory
Less suitable when threads can sleep arbitrarily
failures: A thread parked inside an epoch pinning memory indefinitely
Missing fences on epoch entry/exit causing UAF
whenNot: Threads may block for long inside critical sections
Hard memory bounds required (prefer hazard pointers)
myths: "Epochs and hazard pointers are interchangeable" — epochs are cheaper but vulnerable to stalled threads
